critic_raw:
This deep RL vs. nearest-neighbor heuristic logistics routing design has a significant preprocessing leakage issue and a fundamental problem with the DRL training architecture.

All preprocessing statistics — including demand aggregation means/stds/quantiles, feature scaling, and historical delivery averages — are computed on the entire 24-month dataset before splitting. The design explicitly states this and acknowledges it as 'potential minimal data leakage.' This framing is incorrect: for a temporal split where the test set is months 19-24, fitting scaling parameters on the full 24 months means that the distributions of features like demand volume, fuel efficiency, and on-time delivery rate from the test period are used to normalize training-period features. For time-series delivery data where demand and efficiency may be non-stationary, this constitutes meaningful leakage. The 'minimal' characterization is asserted without evidence.

The DRL agent is trained with PPO for 50 epochs on historical data. PPO is an on-policy algorithm that requires fresh environment interactions to compute valid policy gradient estimates. Training PPO on a fixed historical dataset without an interactive environment means the agent cannot collect on-policy rollouts — it is being trained on an offline dataset with an algorithm designed for online interaction. This is the same fundamental misuse of PPO as noted for eval_scenario_717: the convergence guarantees of PPO do not apply, and the training procedure may produce an unstable or suboptimal policy that appears to work on the training distribution but fails in the test period.

The baseline is described as 'a traditional nearest-neighbor heuristic with 2-opt local optimization.' This is a reasonable operational baseline. However, the RL agent's action space is discrete route selection from 10-20 candidate routes 'generated by a fast heuristic route generator.' The RL agent's action space is therefore constrained to outputs of the heuristic — it can only select among routes the heuristic proposes. This means the RL agent cannot outperform the heuristic on any route that the heuristic's generator does not produce. The comparison is between 'best heuristic route' and 'RL-selected route from heuristic candidates,' where the ceiling of RL performance is bounded by the heuristic's route generation quality.

The reward function has four components with free parameters α, β, γ, δ that are grid-searched over the validation set. The reward weights are simultaneously hyperparameters of the RL training procedure and implicit preferences about the operational trade-off between delivery time, fuel, on-time performance, and service quality. Tuning reward weights on the validation set optimizes the reward function to produce high performance on validation data, but the resulting trade-off may not reflect actual operational preferences. More critically, tuning the reward weights is equivalent to tuning which objective the primary metric measures — the grid search selects both the model and the objective simultaneously.

The efficiency ratio metric (delivery_time / baseline_expected_time) uses the baseline as a normalization denominator. If the heuristic baseline is used both as the comparison method and as the denominator of the efficiency metric, then an RL agent that matches the heuristic will report efficiency ratio ≈ 1.0 on the test set. The target threshold of <0.95 (5% improvement) is therefore defined relative to the comparison method, making the success criterion asymmetric: beating the heuristic by 5% is measured against the heuristic's own baseline, not an independent reference.
all_issues_raised: All preprocessing statistics including demand aggregation and feature scaling are fit on the full 24-month dataset before splitting, leaking test-period distribution information into training normalization — described as 'minimal' without evidence.

PPO is trained on a fixed historical dataset without an interactive environment, which violates PPO's on-policy requirement for valid policy gradient estimation — the training procedure's convergence properties do not apply in this offline setting.

The RL agent's action space is constrained to routes generated by the heuristic, so the RL agent cannot outperform the heuristic on routes the heuristic's generator does not produce — the upper bound of RL performance is set by the baseline's route generation quality.

Reward function weights (α, β, γ, δ) are grid-searched on the validation set, simultaneously optimizing the model and the implicit operational objective — the selected weights may not reflect actual operator preferences and the model is tuned to a potentially arbitrary trade-off.

The efficiency ratio denominator uses the historical heuristic performance as baseline, making the success criterion (ratio < 0.95) defined relative to the comparison method — a 5% improvement is measured against the heuristic itself rather than an independent reference.
all_issues_adjudicated: All preprocessing statistics including demand aggregation and feature scaling are fit on the full 24-month dataset before splitting, leaking test-period distribution information into training normalization — described as 'minimal' without evidence.

PPO is trained on a fixed historical dataset without an interactive environment, which violates PPO's on-policy requirement for valid policy gradient estimation — the training procedure's convergence properties do not apply in this offline setting.

The RL agent's action space is constrained to routes generated by the heuristic, so the RL agent cannot outperform the heuristic on routes the heuristic's generator does not produce — the upper bound of RL performance is set by the baseline's route generation quality.

Reward function weights (α, β, γ, δ) are grid-searched on the validation set, simultaneously optimizing the model and the implicit operational objective — the selected weights may not reflect actual operator preferences and the model is tuned to a potentially arbitrary trade-off.

The efficiency ratio denominator uses the historical heuristic performance as baseline, making the success criterion (ratio < 0.95) defined relative to the comparison method — a 5% improvement is measured against the heuristic itself rather than an independent reference.
